data.content:
NVIDIA
A company that pioneered accelerated computing and has expanded into various computationally intensive fields, including scientific computing, AI, data science, autonomous vehicles, robotics, and digital twin applications. It is a data center scale AI infrastructure company reshaping industries.<SEP>A company with products and services that function on a standalone basis and are involved in contracts with multiple performance obligations.<SEP>NVIDIA is a company that reports its financial data, including revenue and expenses, and has a focus on Compute & Networking and Graphics segments.

Annual Report on Form 10-K
The Annual Report on Form 10-K is a document that includes NVIDIA's Consolidated Financial Statements, related Notes, and other cautionary statements and risks, which is used to provide a comprehensive view of the company's financial condition and operations.<SEP>The Annual Report on Form 10-K is a comprehensive report filed annually by companies to disclose financial performance and other significant information to investors and regulators.<SEP>The Annual Report on Form 10-K is a document that includes Arista's consolidated financial statements and related notes, as well as forward-looking statements about the company's financial condition and results of operations.<SEP>The Annual Report on Form 10-K is a comprehensive report that includes financial statements and other important information about the company's performance and financial health.<SEP>The Annual Report on Form 10-K is a comprehensive report that includes the company's financial statements and disclosures, including information on accounting policies and tax matters.

Compute & Networking
One of NVIDIA's two operating segments, focused on providing data center compute and networking platforms for accelerated computing and AI solutions.

Graphics
The other operating segment of NVIDIA, related to graphical processing units and technologies.

Santa Clara
The headquarters location of NVIDIA in California.<SEP>Santa Clara is the city in California where Applied Materials, Inc. reported its financial results from.<SEP>Santa Clara is the location in California where land and improvements were purchased for a property project.

California
The state where NVIDIA was originally incorporated.

Delaware
The state where NVIDIA was reincorporated.

Blackwell Architectures
The primary architectures contributing to NVIDIA's Data Center revenue, representing advanced technology in AI infrastructure.

Rubin Platform
An advanced architecture introduced by NVIDIA for Data Center compute, shipped on a one-year product cadence.

Blackwell Ultra
A platform including GB300, which NVIDIA began shipping in the second quarter of fiscal year 2026.

H20 Product
A product by NVIDIA for which a license is required for exports into the China market, leading to a significant charge due to diminished demand.

H200 Products
Products by NVIDIA that can be shipped to specific China-based customers under a new license, subject to a 25% tariff upon importation into the United States.

USG
The United States Government, which has granted licenses for NVIDIA to ship certain products to China-based customers.

China
China is a significant market and participant in global trade, contributing to the net revenue of various companies, including a 30% share in 2025. It is a major location for the expansion of display manufacturing facilities and a substantial portion of customers and suppliers are based there. China plays a crucial role in the supply chain due to its export of certain technologies, materials, and rare earth minerals, which were subject to export controls in 2025. The country faces restrictions on exporting certain products, impacting companies like NVIDIA and their operations, due to United States government export regulations. China is also a geopolitical entity that may contribute to heightened risks of cybersecurity attacks due to ongoing tensions and conflicts. Companies must ensure compliance with export controls when shipping products to China.

AI Infrastructure
The infrastructure that supports AI capabilities, which NVIDIA is building out with its customers and partners, and for which there is a crucial need for data centers, energy, and capital.

Open-source AI
Advanced AI capabilities made broadly accessible through high-quality open-source foundation models, which could affect NVIDIA's product and service demand if deployed on competitors' platforms.

Latin America
A region, along with the U.S., into which NVIDIA is expanding its supply chain to strengthen it and meet growing demand for AI infrastructure.

Consolidated Financial Statements
Financial statements that include NVIDIA's financial data and are part of the Annual Report on Form 10-K.<SEP>The Consolidated Financial Statements are a comprehensive compilation of the company's financial data, including various notes that provide detailed information on different aspects of the company's financial health.<SEP>Consolidated Financial Statements are financial reports included in the Form 10-K that provide a comprehensive view of the company's financial position, along with the Notes to Consolidated Financial Statements.><SEP>The Consolidated Financial Statements are comprehensive financial reports that include detailed notes on the company's credit facilities and debt.<SEP>The Consolidated Financial Statements are a comprehensive report of a company's financial position, including assets, liabilities, equity, income, and expenses. They are based on historical and anticipated results, trends, and various assumptions.

data.entity_name: Notes to the Consolidated Financial Statements
data.content:
Notes to the Consolidated Financial Statements
Notes that provide additional information related to NVIDIA's Consolidated Financial Statements.<SEP>The Notes to the Consolidated Financial Statements provide further discussion related to borrowing facilities, debt obligations, guarantees, commitments, and contingencies.

GB300
A specific product within the Blackwell Ultra platforms that NVIDIA began shipping in the second quarter of fiscal year 2026.

AI Model Makers
AI model makers are customers of NVIDIA, either direct or indirect, involved in the creation and deployment of AI models.<SEP>Early-stage startups that purchase NVIDIA's products directly or through CSPs and are part of the AI ecosystem.

Hopper HGX
Hopper HGX systems are a type of technology product that the company transitioned away from offering in their business model.

Blackwell
Blackwell is a product associated with strong demand, impacting revenue in Gaming and Professional Visualization sectors.<SEP>Blackwell full-scale datacenter solutions are the new focus of the company's business model.

H20
H20 is associated with excess inventory and purchase obligations that impacted the company's financials.

Provisions for Inventory and Excess Inventory Purchase Obligations
Provisions for inventory and excess inventory purchase obligations are financial figures that totaled $7.2 billion and $3.7 billion for fiscal years 2026 and 2025, respectively.

Sales of Previously Reserved Inventory

data.entity_name: Settlements of Excess Inventory Purchase Obligations
data.content:
Settlements of Excess Inventory Purchase Obligations
Settlements of excess inventory purchase obligations resulted in a provision release of $689 million for fiscal year 2025.

Research and Development
Research and development expenses for fiscal year 2026 were $18,497 million, showing a 43% increase from the previous year.<SEP>Research and development efforts focused on creating new products and technologies, critical to the company's future success.<SEP>Research and development is a core business function critical for future success, involving significant investment in new products and technology development.<SEP>Research and Development is a category of expenses that increased by 5% for the company in the fiscal year ended March 31, 2025.<SEP>Research and Development refers to the company's policies and activities related to innovation and intellectual property.<SEP>Research and Development expenses are costs incurred in creating new products or improving existing ones.<SEP>Research and development expenses increased by $141.8 million, primarily due to increased personnel costs and new product introduction costs.

Sales, General and Administrative
Sales, general and administrative expenses for fiscal year 2026 were $4,579 million, showing a 31% increase from the previous year.

Total Operating Expenses
Total operating expenses for fiscal year 2026 were $23,076 million, showing a 41% increase from the previous year.

Interest Income
Interest income for fiscal year 2026 was $2,300 million, showing an increase from the previous year.<SEP>Interest income consists of interest received on cash, cash equivalents, short-term investments, and loans receivable.<SEP>Interest Income is a category that increased by 5% for the company in the fiscal year ended March 31, 2025.<SEP>Interest Income is the revenue generated from interest on investments, loans, or accounts receivable.<SEP>Interest income for the year ended December 31, 2025, was $383.4 million, a 23.3% increase from the previous year, due to higher cash and marketable securities balances.<SEP>Interest income increased by $158.6 million, due to higher cash and marketable securities balances and investment yields.

Interest Expense
Interest expense for fiscal year 2026 was $(259) million, showing a slight increase from the previous year.<SEP>Interest Expense is the cost of borrowing money, amounting to $69 in 2026 and $64 in 2025.

Other Income, Net
Other income, net for fiscal year 2026 was $9,022 million, showing a significant increase from the previous year.

Total Other Income, Net
Total other income, net for fiscal year 2026 was $11,063 million, showing an increase from the previous year.<SEP>Total other income, net for the year ended December 31, 2025, was $393.6 million, a 22.8% increase from the previous year.

Income Tax Expense
Income tax expense was $21.4 billion for fiscal year 2026 and $11.1 billion for fiscal year 2025.

One Big Beautiful Bill Act
The One Big Beautiful Bill Act (OBBBA) was enacted into law in July 2025 and contains changes to key U.S. federal income tax laws.<SEP>The One Big Beautiful Bill Act is legislation that has affected the realization of CAMT credits and the valuation of deferred tax assets.<SEP>The One Big Beautiful Bill Act is legislation that has affected the realization of corporate alternative minimum tax credits.><SEP>The One Big Beautiful Bill Act, enacted on July 4, 2025, includes tax reform provisions such as full expensing of domestic research costs and increasing the investment tax credit for certain investments in domestic semiconductor manufacturing from 25% to 35%.<SEP>The One Big Beautiful Bill Act is a legislation that affects the tax deductions for U.S. tax purposes and influences the organization's tax planning and valuation allowances.<SEP>The One Big Beautiful Bill Act (OBBBA) is a legislation enacted by the U.S. government that affects the organization's ability to use deferred tax assets.

Net Cash Used in Financing Activities
Net cash used in financing activities was $(48,474) million for fiscal year 2026.

Cash and Cash Equivalents
Cash and cash equivalents were $10,605 million as of January 25, 2026.<SEP>Cash and Cash Equivalents is a component of total cash, cash equivalents, and restricted cash, with amounts of 7,218 and 6,264.

Marketable Securities
Marketable securities were $51,951 million as of January 25, 2026.<SEP>Marketable securities include publicly-held equity securities and debt securities issued by various entities.<SEP>Marketable Securities represents the company's investment portfolio primarily invested in highly-rated securities with the objective of minimizing the potential risk of principal loss.<SEP>Marketable Securities are financial instruments that can be readily bought or sold in the market and are often held as part of a company's investment portfolio.

data.entity_name: Cash, Cash Equivalents, and Marketable Securities
data.content:
Cash, Cash Equivalents, and Marketable Securities
Cash, cash equivalents, and marketable securities totaled $62,556 million as of January 25, 2026.<SEP>The total balance of cash, cash equivalents, and marketable securities was $10.7 billion as of December 31, 2025.

FDDEI
FDDEI refers to the Foreign Derived Intangible Income, which is a tax concept impacting the company's effective tax rate.

U.S. Federal Research Tax Credit
The U.S. federal research tax credit is a tax benefit that lowers the company's effective tax rate.

Publicly-Held Equity Securities
Publicly-held equity securities are financial instruments whose value can significantly fluctuate due to changes in stock prices, affecting financial results.

Commercial Arrangements
Commercial arrangements refer to agreements made to support the build-out of complex datacenter infrastructures, including guarantees with partners.

Early-Stage Companies
Early-stage companies are businesses in their initial phase that receive land, power, and shell guarantees, generally over multi-year periods.

Escrow
Escrow is a process used in transactions where funds or assets are held by a third party until certain conditions are met.

Third Parties
Third parties are external entities that may be involved in subleases of underlying leases if partners' operating activities are insufficient.<SEP>Third Parties are external entities that may be affected by the announcement or consummation of a proposed transaction.

Macroeconomic Factors
Macroeconomic factors include tariffs, inflation, interest rate changes, capital market volatility, global supply chain constraints, and geopolitical developments, impacting operations and financial results.

Supply Chain
The supply chain is a network of organizations involved in the production and distribution of products, affected by macroeconomic factors.<SEP>The supply chain refers to the network of entities involved in the production and distribution of products, including manufacturing, sourcing, and logistics.<SEP>The supply chain is the process managed by the company to ensure reliable inventory deployment and balance customer requirements against supplier lead times and availability.

Fiscal Year 2026 Summary
The Fiscal Year 2026 Summary is a financial report detailing the company's performance, including revenue, gross margin, operating income, and net income.

Revenue
Revenue for fiscal year 2026 was $215.9 billion, showing a 65% increase from the previous year.<SEP>Revenue is a financial metric representing the income generated from business operations, amounting to $7,012 in 2026 and $7,166 in 2025.<SEP>Total revenue for the year ended December 31, 2024, was $7,003.1 million, a 19.5% increase from the previous year.<SEP>Revenue is a financial metric that represents the total income generated from the company's operations, which increased to $9,005.7 million in 2025, up by 28.6% from $7,003.1 million in 2024.

Data Center
Data Center revenue for fiscal year 2026 increased by 68%, driven by platform shifts in computing and AI.

Gaming
Gaming revenue for fiscal year 2026 increased by 41%, driven by Blackwell demand, with supply constraints expected to impact the first quarter of fiscal 2027 and beyond.

Professional Visualization
Professional Visualization revenue for fiscal year 2026 increased by 70%, driven by demand for Blackwell and the launch of DGX Spark.

Automotive
Automotive revenue for fiscal year 2026 increased by 39%, driven by the continued adoption of self-driving platforms.<SEP>Automotive is a sector that uses semiconductor products for various automotive technologies and systems.>

Hopper HGX Systems
Hopper HGX systems are part of the business model that transitioned to Blackwell full-scale datacenter solutions, impacting gross margin.<SEP>The Hopper HGX systems are a previous generation of products offered by NVIDIA, which they transitioned away from in favor of Blackwell full-scale datacenter solutions.

DGX Spark
DGX Spark is a new product launch that contributed to the increase in Professional Visualization revenue.

U.S. GAAP
U.S. GAAP stands for accounting principles generally accepted in the United States, which the company follows for financial statement preparation.<SEP>U.S. GAAP refers to Generally Accepted Accounting Principles in the United States, which guide the preparation of financial statements.<SEP>U.S. GAAP stands for Generally Accepted Accounting Principles in the United States, which are the accounting standards used for the preparation of financial statements.

Inventories
Inventories are tracked to manage cost of sales for inventory provisions and to write-down obsolete or excess inventory.

Form 10-K
Form 10-K is an annual report filed by publicly traded companies with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC). It provides a comprehensive summary of a company's financial performance, including significant accounting policies, financial statements, and outstanding shares for a fiscal year. The report contains detailed information on the company's financial performance, including income taxes, as well as notes to the consolidated financial statements. Form 10-K serves as a crucial document for investors and stakeholders to understand a company's financial health and make informed decisions. It is required by the SEC for all publicly traded companies to ensure transparency and accountability in the financial reporting process.

H20 Excess Inventory
The H20 excess inventory and purchase obligations resulted in a $4.5 billion charge for NVIDIA in the first quarter of fiscal year 2026, affecting the company's operating income.<SEP>H20 excess inventory refers to the overstock of H20 products that led to a $4.5 billion charge, impacting the gross margin.

Purchase Obligations
Purchase obligations are commitments to buy certain products or services in the future, which, when associated with H20 excess inventory, led to a significant charge.<SEP>Purchase Obligations amount to $10.6 billion for goods and services as of October 26, 2025.<SEP>Purchase Obligations are non-cancelable commitments the company has made for cloud computing web services, data centers, software, licenses, and services.

Third-Party Manufacturers
Entities responsible for manufacturing products with lead times and constraints that can extend beyond twelve months for some products.

U.S.
The United States (U.S.) is a significant location for the company's business operations, where it is subject to federal and state income taxes. The company holds cash, cash equivalents, and marketable securities in the U.S., which is also a primary location for its business operations. Customers in the U.S. contribute to the company's revenue, making it a crucial market for the organization. The U.S. government implements trade and national security policies, including export controls that can impact the business dealings of the IPLA and Arm China. The country is also subject to complex tax laws and regulations, which are applied to the company's operations. Furthermore, the U.S. is one of the countries that have implemented or are considering implementing changes to tax and accounting laws in line with Pillar Two. The OBBB Act was signed into law in the U.S., where the company conducts business and is subject to local and federal taxes. Overall, the U.S. plays a vital role in the company's financial and operational activities, with a significant portion of its cash and marketable securities held in the country.

Deferred Tax Assets and Liabilities
Assets and liabilities calculated based on estimates and judgments, subject to changes in tax laws and accounting standards.

Valuation Allowance
A measure to reduce deferred tax assets to the amount believed more likely to be realized.<SEP>Valuation Allowance is recorded against deferred tax assets when it is more likely than not that some portion or all of the assets will not be realized.<SEP>Valuation Allowance is an accounting concept used to account for the impairment of deferred tax assets, reflecting the company's ability to realize these benefits in the future.

Non-Marketable Equity Securities
Investments in private companies without readily determinable fair values, measured at cost minus impairment.

Revenue Allowances
Reductions to revenue for estimated product returns and customer programs, based on historical return rates and expected claims.

Multiple Performance Obligations
Contracts that contain more than one performance obligation, requiring judgment to determine distinctness for revenue recognition.

License and Development Arrangements
A type of contract where products and services do not function on a standalone basis and require significant integration or interdependency.

Standalone Selling Price
The price at which products or services are sold separately in comparable circumstances to similar customers, used for revenue recognition.

Consolidated Statements of Income
A financial report that includes certain items expressed as a percentage of revenue, comparing fiscal years 2026 and 2025.

SEC
The U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission, where the company's Annual Report on Form 10-K is filed.<SEP>The SEC is mentioned in relation to ARM's obligation to prepare and distribute periodic reports.

Income Tax Contingencies
Potential liabilities recognized for U.S. and foreign income tax based on estimates of additional taxes that may be due.

Interest and Penalties
Components of income tax expense related to unrecognized tax benefits.<SEP>Interest and penalties related to uncertain tax positions amounting to $118 million as of October 26, 2025, are part of the company's financial obligations.

Revenue Recognition
The process of recording revenue, which includes establishing allowances for sales returns and accruing for customer programs.

Sales Return Allowance
A reduction to revenue for estimated product returns based on historical return rates.

Customer Programs
Programs involving rebates and marketing development funds, accounted for as a reduction in revenue.

Distributor Price Incentives
Incentives provided to distributors as part of customer programs, requiring judgment on whether the incentives will be attained.

Relative Standalone Selling Price
The method used to allocate the total transaction price to each distinct performance obligation when direct observable prices are not available.

Groq
Groq is an organization that entered into a non-exclusive license agreement.

U.S. government
The U.S. government is an entity that issues debt securities.<SEP>The U.S. government is responsible for enacting tax laws, including the One Big Beautiful Bill Act (OBBBA), which impacts the organization's tax provisions.

Board of Directors
The Board of Directors is the entity that approved additional share repurchase authorization.<SEP>The Board of Directors is a group of individuals elected by shareholders to oversee the management of the company and make key decisions.<SEP>The Board of Directors is a governing body responsible for making decisions regarding the payment of dividends and share repurchases, considering the best interest of stockholders.<SEP>The Board of Directors is an organizational body responsible for overseeing the company's major risks, including cybersecurity risks.<SEP>The Board of Directors is the entity that determines the fair value of ordinary shares for share-based awards.

OpenAI
OpenAI is an organization with which the company is finalizing an investment and partnership agreement.

U.S. Inflation Reduction Act of 2022
The U.S. Inflation Reduction Act of 2022 is legislation that requires a 1% excise tax on certain share repurchases.

Equity Investment Securities
Equity investment securities are a type of financial instrument that the company purchased.

Share Repurchase Authorization
Share repurchase authorization is the approval by the Board of Directors to buy back company shares.

Common Stock
Common stock represents the financial instrument that the company repurchases in the market.<SEP>Common Stock refers to the class of ownership in a company that provides investors with voting rights and potential for capital appreciation.<SEP>Common stock represents the class of equity security that represents ownership in a company and is subject to market price fluctuations.

Cash Dividends
Cash dividends are financial transactions where the company pays out cash to its shareholders.

Commercial Paper Program
The commercial paper program is a financial program that allows the company to issue unsecured commercial paper notes.

Blackwell Computing Platform
The Blackwell computing platform is a product that experienced a 59% growth in revenue from Data Center computing, contributing to NVIDIA's overall revenue increase.

NVLink Compute Fabric
The NVLink compute fabric is a product introduced by NVIDIA for GB200 and GB300 systems, which contributed to a 142% growth in Data Center networking revenue.

Compute & Networking Segment
The Compute & Networking segment of NVIDIA, which includes sales of the Blackwell computing platform and NVLink compute fabric, experienced significant revenue growth.

Graphics Segment
The Graphics segment of NVIDIA, which includes sales of the Blackwell architecture, experienced growth in revenue and operating income.

AIBs
AIBs are direct customers of NVIDIA, purchasing products such as GPUs and other computing solutions.

ODMs
ODMs are direct customers of NVIDIA, involved in the original design and manufacturing of products using NVIDIA's technology.

OEMs
OEMs are direct customers of NVIDIA, producing products that incorporate NVIDIA's technology.

CSPs
CSPs are both direct and indirect customers of NVIDIA, offering cloud services and purchasing products for cloud infrastructure.

Neocloud Builders
Neocloud builders are indirect customers of NVIDIA, involved in the construction of cloud services and infrastructure.

Enterprises
Enterprises are indirect customers of NVIDIA, purchasing products through direct customers for their business operations.

Public Sector Entities
Public sector entities are indirect customers of NVIDIA, involved in the procurement of technology for public services.

United States
The United States is a significant economic and political entity with a substantial impact on global trade and business. It serves as a base for many of NVIDIA's direct customers, contributing to 31% of the company's total revenue in fiscal year 2026. Additionally, the United States contributed an 11% share to the net revenue of a company in 2025. The country is also a primary location for companies like AMAT to conduct product development and manufacturing activities.

The United States engages in global trade and has implemented various trade policies and export regulations that affect businesses. Its trade policies, including tariffs, have a direct impact on the business landscape, with a considerable number of customers and suppliers located outside the country. Furthermore, the United States is a potential adopter of laws and regulations related to AI, which may influence the use of AI in product development.

The country is a jurisdiction where companies are subject to income taxes, and tax laws can significantly impact their tax liabilities. Companies operating in the United States are also subject to a range of laws and regulations, including those related to financial disclosures, securities, and corporate governance. Overall, the United States plays a crucial role in the global economy and business operations, with its policies and regulations having far-reaching implications.

Blackwell Full-Scale Datacenter Solutions
The Blackwell full-scale datacenter solutions represent a new product line from NVIDIA, contributing to a change in their business model and affecting gross margins.

Gross Profit
Gross profit is a financial metric representing total net revenue less the cost of revenue, which decreased from 75.0% to 71.1% for NVIDIA in fiscal year 2026.<SEP>Gross Profit is the profit after deducting the cost of goods sold from revenue, amounting to $3,435 in 2026 and $3,496 in 2025.<SEP>Gross profit for the year ended December 31, 2024, was $4,491.3 million, a 23.7% increase from the previous year.<SEP>Gross profit is a financial metric that represents the profit a company makes after deducting the costs associated with producing and selling its products, which increased to $5,768.7 million in 2025, up by 28.4% from $4,491.3 million in 2024.

Gross Margin
Gross margin is a financial ratio that decreased for NVIDIA from 75.0% to 71.1% in fiscal year 2026 due to a change in business model and a significant charge related to H20 inventory.<SEP>Gross margin is a financial metric representing the company's profitability, which has remained at 64.1%, indicating the company's efficiency in managing costs.<SEP>Gross margin is the percentage of revenue left after deducting the cost of revenue, which remained constant at 64.1% for the years ended December 31, 2025 and 2024.<SEP>Gross margin for the year ended December 31, 2024, was 64.1%, slightly higher than the previous year's 61.9%.<SEP>Gross margin increased from 61.9% in 2023 to 64.1% in 2024, reflecting improved product margins.

Fiscal Year 2026
Fiscal Year 2026 is the most recent fiscal year for which NVIDIA reported financial data, including a decrease in gross margins and revenue growth in specific segments.<SEP>Fiscal Year 2026 is a period during which the company spent $6.1 billion on capital expenditures.<SEP>Fiscal Year 2026 is the financial year starting on or after July 1, 2025, and ending on or before June 30, 2026, relevant for the implementation of new accounting standards.

Fiscal Year 2025
Fiscal Year 2025 is the previous fiscal year for which NVIDIA reported financial data, used as a comparative period for analyzing changes in revenue and expenses.<SEP>Fiscal Year 2025 is a period during which the company spent $3.4 billion on capital expenditures.

Note 10
Note 10 of the Consolidated Financial Statements contains descriptions of facility lease guarantees.

Note 11
Note 11 of the Consolidated Financial Statements contains descriptions of long-term debt.

Note 12
Note 12 of the Consolidated Financial Statements contains descriptions of purchase commitments.

Note 17
Note 17 of the Consolidated Financial Statements contains descriptions of operating lease obligations.

Item 1A
Item 1A of the report contains additional information regarding the company's investments and risk factors.

Fiscal Year 2027
Fiscal Year 2027 is a period in which the company expects to increase capital expenditures relative to fiscal year 2026.<SEP>Fiscal Year 2027 is the financial year starting on or after July 1, 2026, and ending on or before June 30, 2027, relevant for the implementation of new accounting standards.

Unrecognized Tax Benefits
Unrecognized Tax Benefits of $4.0 billion include related interest and penalties of $374 million, recorded in non-current income tax payable at the end of fiscal year 2026.<SEP>The Unrecognized Tax Benefits with a gross liability of $452 million as of October 26, 2025, are not expected to result in payment of cash within one year.<SEP>Unrecognized Tax Benefits are potential tax positions that the company has not yet recognized in financial statements due to uncertainties regarding tax audits and outcomes.

Internal Revenue Service
The Internal Revenue Service is an organization currently examining the company's fiscal years 2023 and 2024.

Note 13
Note 13 of the Consolidated Financial Statements contains further information regarding unrecognized tax benefits.

Item 15
Item 15 of Part IV of the Annual Report on Form 10-K contains the Notes to the Consolidated Financial Statements.

Note 1
Note 1 of the Consolidated Financial Statements discusses the adoption of new and recently issued accounting pronouncements.

Part IV
Part IV of the Annual Report on Form 10-K contains specific sections, including Item 15, which houses the Notes to the Consolidated Financial Statements.

Financial Results
The fluctuation in value of publicly-held equity securities can adversely affect financial results.

Complex Datacenter Infrastructures
Commercial arrangements are made to support the build-out of complex datacenter infrastructures.

Employee Growth
Operating expenses increased due to higher compensation and benefits expenses resulting from employee growth.

Non-Cancellable Inventory Orders
Third-Party Manufacturers' lead times affect the company's decision to place non-cancellable inventory orders.

Operating Expenses
Operating expenses increased due to higher compensation and benefits expenses resulting from employee growth.<SEP>Operating Expenses include all expenses involved in the operations of a company, totaling $1,604 in 2026 and $1,321 in 2025.<SEP>Operating Expenses include the costs of running the company's operations, such as research and development, selling, general, and administrative expenses.<SEP>Operating expenses include research and development, sales and marketing, and general and administrative expenses, which increased by $365.9 million, or 23.7%, for the year ended December 31, 2025 compared to 2024.

Income Taxes
Deferred Tax Assets and Liabilities are part of the company's income tax calculations in the U.S. and foreign jurisdictions.<SEP>Income Taxes are a concept referring to the taxes that the company must pay to the U.S. and foreign jurisdictions based on its income.

Deferred Tax Assets
The Valuation Allowance is used to reduce the Deferred Tax Assets to the amount more likely to be realized.<SEP>Deferred tax assets are financial figures representing the tax benefits of future deductions that are recognized in accounting due to temporary differences between book income and taxable income.<SEP>Deferred Tax Assets are recognized for the estimated future tax effects of temporary differences and for net operating loss and tax credit carryovers.<SEP>Deferred tax assets are a financial consideration for AMD, which may be realized or result in tax liabilities.<SEP>Deferred Tax Assets are a concept in accounting that represents the tax benefits that can be utilized in the future against taxable income. They require valuation allowances to be assessed for potential impairment.

Investments
Non-Marketable Equity Securities are part of the company's investments in private companies.<SEP>Investments are financial commitments that can impact AMD's business.

Contracts
Contracts with Multiple Performance Obligations require distinctness determination for revenue recognition.

Operating Income
The Operating Income is a key item in the Consolidated Statements of Income, representing the company's financial performance.

Cash, Cash Equivalents, Marketable Securities
The company holds cash, cash equivalents, and marketable securities outside the U.S.

Investment and Partnership Agreement
The company is finalizing an investment and partnership agreement with OpenAI.

Excise Tax
The U.S. Inflation Reduction Act of 2022 includes an excise tax on certain share repurchases, affecting the company's share repurchase cost.

Cash used in investing activities
The increase in cash used in investing activities is primarily due to higher purchases of equity investment securities.

Capital Return to Shareholders
The company repurchased 282 million shares of its common stock as part of its capital return to shareholders.

Shareholders
In fiscal year 2026, the company paid cash dividends of $974 million to its shareholders.

Capital Equipment
Capital equipment refers to the equipment at customer sites used for patterning, transistor, interconnect, process control, and packaging performance.

AGS Segment
The AGS segment is a part of a company that provides services, spares, and factory automation software globally to customer fabrication plants, optimizing the performance of semiconductor and other equipment.

Semiconductor Systems Segment
The Semiconductor Systems segment is a part of a company that will take over the 200mm equipment business from the AGS segment starting in the first quarter of fiscal 2026.<SEP>The Semiconductor Systems segment is a part of a company that contributes significantly to the net revenue, representing the largest share.><SEP>The Semiconductor Systems segment is a part of the company that derives a significant portion of the company's net revenue and profitability from the sale of equipment used to fabricate semiconductor chips.

Corporate and Other Category
The Corporate and Other category includes revenues and costs of products not included in reportable segments and certain operating expenses managed separately at the corporate level.

United States Government
